import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
Show the code 
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Include and execute your code here
df = pl.read_csv("https://posit.byui.edu/content/0d43d66f-d3b7-4c43-8c36-52e3ed69e7f1/names_year_csv.csv")

QUESTION 1

How does your name at your birth year compare to its use historically? Your must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.

I was named “Nathanael” at the height of the popularity of the name.

Show the code 
# Q1
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

my_name = "Nathanael"
my_birth_year = 2004

name_df = (
    df.filter(pl.col("name") == my_name)
      .select(["year", "Total"])
      .sort("year")
)

birth_year_total = name_df.filter(pl.col("year") == my_birth_year)["Total"][0]



years = name_df["year"].to_list()
totals = name_df["Total"].to_list()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))
plt.plot(years, totals)
plt.axvline(x=my_birth_year, color="red", linestyle="--")
plt.scatter([my_birth_year], [birth_year_total], color="red", zorder=5)

plt.title(f'Historical usage of the name "{my_name}"')
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Total babies")
plt.xticks(years[::5], [str(y) for y in years[::5]], rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

QUESTION 2

Think of a unique name from a famous movie. Plot the usage of that name and see how changes line up with the movie release. Does it look like the movie had an effect on usage?

Yes. There is a noticable spike at 2005, the year that Revenge of the Sith was released. Interestingly, there was a large drop in 2019, the year Rise of Skywalker released.

Show the code 
# Q2
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

name = "Anakin"
movie_year = 2005

name_df = (
    df.filter(pl.col("name") == name)
      .select(["year", "Total"])
      .sort("year")
)

years = name_df["year"].to_list()
totals = name_df["Total"].to_list()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))
plt.plot(years, totals)
plt.axvline(x=movie_year, color="red", linestyle="--")

plt.title('Historical usage of the name "Anakin"')
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Total babies")
plt.xticks(years, [str(y) for y in years], rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

QUESTION 3

Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul are all Christian names. From 1920 - 2000, compare the name usage of each of the four names in a single chart. What trends do you notice?

All of those names have dropped in popularity over the last 70 years.

Show the code 
# Q3
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

names = ["Mary", "Martha", "Peter", "Paul"]

name_df = (
    df.filter(pl.col("name").is_in(names))
      .filter((pl.col("year") >= 1920) & (pl.col("year") <= 2000))
      .select(["name", "year", "Total"])
      .sort("year")
)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

for name in names:
    filtered = name_df.filter(pl.col("name") == name)
    years = filtered["year"].to_list()
    totals = filtered["Total"].to_list()
    plt.plot(years, totals, label=name)

plt.title("Christian Name Usage (1920-2000)")
plt.xlabel("Year")
plt.ylabel("Total babies")

all_years = name_df["year"].unique().sort().to_list()
plt.xticks(all_years[::5], [str(y) for y in all_years[::5]], rotation=45)

plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
